title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption: Between 1980 and 2010, the gender wage ratio in the United States shows a general upward trend across the tenth, fiftieth, and ninetieth income percentiles. The lines for the tenth and fiftieth percentiles rise steadily from starting points near 60% and 65% respectively, converging to reach approximately 82% by 2010. The ninetieth percentile line begins slightly higher than the tenth percentile in 1980, rises to a peak around 75% in 1990, and then flattens out, remaining below the other two lines for the remainder of the period. By the end of the timeline, the gap between the median earners and the lowest earners has narrowed significantly, while the ratio for the highest earners has remained relatively static since 1990. The data shows that while wage ratios improved for lower and middle income groups over these three decades, the ratio for the top ten percent of earners plateaued after an initial increase.
